overview: Written in 1891, this was Bialik's first published poem and it established him as the "National Poet." It was inspired by the Hebrew Enlightenment (Haskalah) and the early Zionist movement. The poem uses a bird returning from the "warm lands" (the Land of Israel) to the poet's cold window in Russia as a messenger. It is an expression of the pain of the Diaspora, asking the bird if the land of the fathers is as beautiful as the ancient texts promise, or if it, too, suffers from hardship.
poem_lines: Greetings upon your return, sweet bird,
From the warm countries to my window—
How my soul longed for your pleasant voice
During the winter when you left my home.
Sing, tell me, my dear bird,
Of wonders from the land of far-away,
Is it also there, in that warm, beautiful land,
That troubles and hardships abound?
Do you bear me greetings from my brothers in Zion,
From my brothers who are distant yet close?
O happy ones! Do they know how I suffer,
Suffer from grief, and have no rest?
Do they know how many enemies I have,
How many rise against me to destroy me?
Sing, my bird, of the land of my fathers,
Where the air is life and the soul is light.
Does the dew fall like pearls on Mount Hermon,
Or does it descend and fall like tears?
And what of the Jordan and its clear waters?
And what of the mountains and the valleys?
Has the glory of Lebanon been brought low?
Has the crown of its beauty fallen?
Sing, my bird, for my soul is weary,
Tell me of the land where my hope resides.
Is the valley of Sharon still blooming with roses?
Is the lily of the valleys still white?
The days of my life are like shadows passing,
Darkness and gloom have filled my heart.
But your song, my bird, brings me a spark of light,
A memory of the morning, a breath of the spring.
Sing then, sing on, my sweet bird,
Until the shadows flee and the light returns.
poem_hebrew_lines: אֶל קוֹלֵךְ כִּי עָרֵב מַה נַּפְשִׁי כָלָתָה
הֲיָרַד כְּפִנִינִים הַטַּל עַל הַר חֶרְמוֹן,
אַךְ שִׁירֵךְ, צִפּוֹרִי, נִיצוֹץ אוֹר מֵבִיא,
